perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed by or on behalf of a party (“Discloser”) and/or its Affiliates during the term of this Agreement which is received, learned, or observed by the other party (“Recipient”) and/or its Affiliates is “Confidential Information,” including, but not limited to, business and financial information, marketing and strategic plans, customer lists, employee lists, reports, technologies, proc esses and operations, compilations, analyses, forecasts, studies, summaries, notes, data, product designs, drawings, prints, specifications, production schedules, test data, operations, inventions, know -how, component and part designs, schematics, formulat ions, samples, passwords, machines, network IDs, prototypes, mock -ups, templates, processing and control information, product performance data, manuals, purchase and sales records, warranty and repair records, marketing information, quotes and pricing info rmation, computer programs, or the like, including e- mail and electronic communication discussing any of the foregoing. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Confidential Information shall not include any information that is: (a) already lawfully in the possession of or known to Recipient as of the Effective Date without any obligation of confidentiality; (b) already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to Recipient or which, after such disclosure, enters into the public domain through no fault of Recipient; (c) lawfully furnished or disclosed to Recipient by a non- party to this Agreement without any obligation of confidentiality and through no wrongful act of Recipient; (d) independently developed by Recipient without the use of any Confidential Informati on; or (e) explicitly approved for public release by Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: All information disclosed by or on behalf of a party (“Discloser”) and/or its Affiliates during the term of this Agreement which is received, learned, or observed by the other party (“Recipient”) and/or its Affiliates is “Confidential Information,” including, but not limited to, business and financial information clearly marked as such, marketing and strategic plans, customer lists, employee lists, reports, technologies, proc esses and operations, compilations, analyses, forecasts, studies, summaries, notes, data, product designs, drawings, prints, specifications, production schedules, test data, operations, inventions, know -how, component and part designs, schematics, formulat ions, samples, passwords, machines, network IDs, prototypes, mock -ups, templates, processing and control information, product performance data, manuals, purchase and sales records, warranty and repair records, marketing information, quotes and pricing info rmation, computer programs, or the like, including e- mail and electronic communication discussing any of the foregoing. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Confidential Information shall not include any information that is: (a) already lawfully in the possession of or known to Recipient as of the Effective Date without any obligation of confidentiality; (b) already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to Recipient or which, after such disclosure, enters into the public domain through no fault of Recipient; (c) lawfully furnished or disclosed to Recipient by a non- party to this Agreement without any obligation of confidentiality and through no wrongful act of Recipient; (d) independently developed by Recipient without the use of any Confidential Informati on; or (e) explicitly approved for public release by Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'clearly marked as such' to the definition of confidential information in Section 2, we create a contradiction with Section 6. Section 6 requires all forms of confidential information to be returned regardless of whether they are marked. Thus, some information which meets the general definition of confidential information under section 2 might be excluded, if not clearly marked as such, while Section 6 requires all confidential information to be returned. This leads to ambiguity about what must be returned at termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: RETURN OF MATERIALS. All forms of Confidential Information, tangible and electronic, and all copies thereof, shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of Discloser, and shall be promptly returned or destroyed by Recipient upon the earlier of the written request of Discloser or the termination of this Agreement. Except as noted in the immediately following sentence, Confidential Information contained in electronically archived notes, reports, correspondences, emails, and other business records prepared by Recipient in connection with the Purpose under this Agreement need not be returned or destroyed, but shall be subject to all other terms of this Agreement. Confidential Information in the form of schematics, prints, drawings, formulations, and descriptions of processes and know -how, and the like, must be returned to Discloser or destroyed pursuant to this Section 6.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'clearly marked as such' to the definition of confidential information. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text (Section 6), which requires the return of 'all forms of Confidential Information,' regardless of whether they are marked. This introduces ambiguity and a contradiction: information that meets the general definition but isn't 'clearly marked' might not be considered confidential under the changed definition, yet Section 6 mandates its return. This fulfills the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' as the definition of 'Confidential Information' is defined inconsistently.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall take reasonable measures to protect Confidential Information from misuse and unauthorized disclosure, but in no event less than the measures it takes to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall take reasonable measures to protect Confidential Information from misuse and unauthorized disclosure, but in no event less than commercially reasonable measures.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'the measures it takes to protect its own confidential information' to 'commercially reasonable measures' in Section 5, we introduce a contradiction with the implicit requirement in Section 2 that all information disclosed should be treated as confidential regardless of the Recipient's standards. This creates a scenario where the recipient could argue that their commercially reasonable measures are lower than what is required to protect the Discloser's specific information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. All information disclosed by or on behalf of a party (“Discloser”) and/or its Affiliates during the term of this Agreement which is received, learned, or observed by the other party (“Recipient”) and/or its Affiliates is “Confidential Information,” including, but not limited to, business and financial information, marketing and strategic plans, customer lists, employee lists, reports, technologies, proc esses and operations, compilations, analyses, forecasts, studies, summaries, notes, data, product designs, drawings, prints, specifications, production schedules, test data, operations, inventions, know -how, component and part designs, schematics, formulat ions, samples, passwords, machines, network IDs, prototypes, mock -ups, templates, processing and control information, product performance data, manuals, purchase and sales records, warranty and repair records, marketing information, quotes and pricing info rmation, computer programs, or the like, including e- mail and electronic communication discussing any of the foregoing. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Confidential Information shall not include any information that is: (a) already lawfully in the possession of or known to Recipient as of the Effective Date without any obligation of confidentiality; (b) already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to Recipient or which, after such disclosure, enters into the public domain through no fault of Recipient; (c) lawfully furnished or disclosed to Recipient by a non- party to this Agreement without any obligation of confidentiality and through no wrongful act of Recipient; (d) independently developed by Recipient without the use of any Confidential Informati on; or (e) explicitly approved for public release by Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to protect Confidential Information with at least the same measures they use to protect their own. The changed text lowers this standard to 'commercially reasonable measures,' which may be less stringent than the Discloser's required level of confidentiality as detailed in the 'Confidential Information' definition. This creates a direct contradiction, as the Recipient's 'commercially reasonable measures' may not adequately protect all information disclosed, potentially including sensitive data that the Discloser would expect to be treated as confidential based on the broad definition provided in the 'Contradicted Text'.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of this Agreement shall be continuing until the Confidential Information disclosed to Recipient is no longer confidential
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of this Agreement shall be continuing for a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, regardless of whether the Confidential Information disclosed to Recipient is no longer confidential
perturbation.explanation: By defining a fixed term of 3 years for the confidentiality obligations in Section 7, regardless of the information's confidentiality, we create a direct contradiction with the original definition in Section 7 that links the obligation to the ongoing confidential nature of the information. This introduces confusion about the duration of the confidentiality agreement, as Section 7 initially implied an indefinite term for confidentiality obligations as long as the information remained confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: TERM AND TERMINATION. The obligations of this Agreement shall be continuing for a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, regardless of whether the Confidential Information disclosed to Recipient is no longer confidential
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the confidentiality obligations continue as long as the information remains confidential. The changed text specifies a fixed term of 3 years for the confidentiality obligations, regardless of the information's confidentiality. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which also specifies a fixed term of 3 years for confidentiality obligations. Therefore, the changed text introducing the fixed term creates a direct contradiction, because the contradicted section also agrees with the changed text creating the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not make copies of Confidential Information in any manner that obstructs or obscures the confidentiality marking of Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not make copies of Confidential Information except with the prior written consent of Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By altering Section 4 to state 'Recipient shall not make copies of Confidential Information except with the prior written consent of Discloser,' an in-text contradiction is created with Section 2. Section 2 outlines what constitutes confidential information, including but not limited to documents without setting restrictions to copy those documents. In contradiction with Section 4 the recipient now needs consent to make copies of documents.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. All information disclosed by or on behalf of a party (“Discloser”) and/or its Affiliates during the term of this Agreement which is received, learned, or observed by the other party (“Recipient”) and/or its Affiliates is “Confidential Information,” including, but not limited to, business and financial information, marketing and strategic plans, customer lists, employee lists, reports, technologies, proc esses and operations, compilations, analyses, forecasts, studies, summaries, notes, data, product designs, drawings, prints, specifications, production schedules, test data, operations, inventions, know -how, component and part designs, schematics, formulat ions, samples, passwords, machines, network IDs, prototypes, mock -ups, templates, processing and control information, product performance data, manuals, purchase and sales records, warranty and repair records, marketing information, quotes and pricing info rmation, computer programs, or the like, including e- mail and electronic communication discussing any of the foregoing. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Confidential Information shall not include any information that is: (a) already lawfully in the possession of or known to Recipient as of the Effective Date without any obligation of confidentiality; (b) already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to Recipient or which, after such disclosure, enters into the public domain through no fault of Recipient; (c) lawfully furnished or disclosed to Recipient by a non- party to this Agreement without any obligation of confidentiality and through no wrongful act of Recipient; (d) independently developed by Recipient without the use of any Confidential Informati on; or (e) explicitly approved for public release by Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The original text of Section 4 allowed copies of Confidential Information as long as confidentiality markings were preserved. The changed text in Section 4 now prohibits all copies of Confidential Information without the Discloser's prior written consent. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 2, which defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, including various documents and data, but doesn't restrict the recipient from making copies. The change in Section 4 imposes a new restriction on copying, conflicting with the freedom implied in Section 2's definition of confidential information, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement embodies the entire agreement and understanding of the parties and supersedes any and all prior oral agreements, arrangements and understandings relating to the Purpose provided for herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement embodies the entire agreement and understanding of the parties with the exception of any previous side letters that were agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: By changing Section 14, we introduce a contradiction, since now the meaning of 'entire agreement' will be different, and the enforceability of the agreement will be more complex, due to the exception of any previous side letters that were agreed upon.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ENTIRE AGREEMENT. This Agreement embodies the entire agreement and understanding of the parties with the exception of any previous side letters that were agreed upon. This Agreement is to be construed without regard to the party or parties responsible for its drafting or preparation. The section headings and captions contained in this Agreement are for convenience only and do not affect the construction or interpretation of any provision of this Agreement. No amendment, waiver or modification of this Agreement is binding unless it is in a writing that explicitly references this Agreement and is signed by authorized representatives of both parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement embodies the 'entire agreement'. The changed text introduces an exception for 'previous side letters'. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. This creates a contradiction because the agreement is now not the entire agreement, directly conflicting with the initial statement that it was. This is in accordance with the Ambiguities definition.
